text:
THORACIC ONCOLOGY -- ON-TREATMENT FOLLOW-UP NOTE
Cycle 1, Week 4 Assessment -- Phase I/II Clinical Trial

**CHIEF COMPLAINT**
Margaret Yuen is a 64-year-old Chinese-American woman with Stage IIIB (T3N2M0 at diagnosis, now with distant metastatic disease reflecting biologic progression) pulmonary adenocarcinoma, EGFR L858R-mutant, presenting for her cycle 1 week 4 on-trial assessment. She is enrolled on a Phase I/II clinical trial evaluating an investigational EGFR/MET bispecific antibody in combination with a novel anti-PD-L1 agent. She presents today for scheduled on-study safety assessment, toxicity review, and blood work. She is accompanied by her daughter.

**HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS**
Ms. Yuen has a well-established history of EGFR L858R-mutant pulmonary adenocarcinoma diagnosed initially at age 62 as Stage IIIB disease (4.8 cm right upper lobe primary, multi-station N2 nodal involvement at stations 4R and 7 on PET-CT and EBUS). She is a former smoker (20 pack-years, quit approximately 12 years prior to diagnosis). Background comorbidities include hypertension, type 2 diabetes mellitus (well-controlled, HbA1c historically in the 6.8-7.1% range), and mild COPD (GOLD Stage I).

Her oncologic course has been eventful. She initially declined concurrent chemoradiation and was treated with upfront osimertinib (Tagrisso) 80 mg daily as first-line therapy for her unresectable Stage IIIB EGFR-mutant disease, achieving a substantial partial response -- her primary tumor shrank from 4.8 cm to a nadir of 1.8 cm over approximately 9 months, and mediastinal nodes normalized. Unfortunately, at approximately 14 months on osimertinib, she developed systemic and intracranial progression: the RUL mass enlarged to 3.2 cm, right paratracheal nodes re-appeared, a small right pleural effusion developed, and brain MRI revealed two new enhancing brain metastases (right frontal 1.1 cm, right cerebellar hemisphere 0.8 cm). Liquid biopsy and subsequent tissue re-biopsy both confirmed acquired EGFR T790M resistance, and, critically, Foundation One CDx performed on the repeat tissue biopsy prior to trial enrollment demonstrated high-level MET amplification (copy number 6.8) as a co-existing bypass resistance mechanism -- a finding directly relevant to her enrollment on this EGFR/MET bispecific trial.

She underwent stereotactic radiosurgery (SRS) to both brain metastases (right frontal 20 Gy/1 fraction, right cerebellar 22 Gy/1 fraction), tolerated this well, and then proceeded to second-line carboplatin (AUC 5) / pemetrexed (500 mg/m2) / bevacizumab (15 mg/kg) q21 days, completing 4 cycles with a partial response (RUL mass down to 2.4 cm), followed by transition to maintenance pemetrexed/bevacizumab. During maintenance she developed grade 2 peripheral neuropathy from prior carboplatin, managed with duloxetine, and mild bevacizumab-related hypertension managed with addition of amlodipine. At approximately 11 months into second-line therapy (6 months of maintenance), she progressed again: the RUL mass grew to 3.8 cm, new bilateral pulmonary nodules appeared (RLL 0.8 cm, LLL 0.6 cm), and the previously SRS-treated right frontal lesion enlarged to 1.4 cm with surrounding edema -- MRI perfusion and MR spectroscopy favored tumor recurrence over radiation necrosis (elevated rCBV, elevated choline:NAA ratio). She received repeat SRS to the right frontal recurrence (18 Gy/1 fraction, reduced dose given prior irradiation) and was started on a short course of dexamethasone for peritumoral edema, which has now been fully tapered.

Her tumor re-biopsy confirmed: EGFR L858R (VAF 48%), EGFR T790M (VAF 22%, elevated compared to prior ctDNA), MET amplification (CN 6.8), RB1 nonsense mutation newly detected (p.Q670*, VAF 18%), TP53 R248W (VAF 52%), CDK4 and CCNE1 low-level amplifications, and PD-L1 TPS 28% (increased from 15% at diagnosis). No small cell transformation on histology. On the basis of this profile -- specifically the EGFR L858R/T790M co-mutation with MET amplification -- she met eligibility criteria and was enrolled on trial. She received cycle 1, day 1 dosing approximately 4 weeks ago, following informed consent discussion with herself and her daughter.

Today she returns for her scheduled C1W4 visit. Overall, she reports tolerating treatment reasonably well. She notes grade 1 fatigue, present throughout the cycle, which she describes as manageable -- she is able to perform her usual activities of daily living but with somewhat reduced stamina compared to her prior best performance status. She denies any recurrence of infusion-related symptoms since the C1D1 event (mild flushing and mild pruritis during the first infusion, managed with diphenhydramine and brief rate reduction, without hemodynamic compromise). She specifically denies: shortness of breath worsening beyond her known mild COPD baseline, new headaches (she notes the right frontal headache that was present at second progression has significantly improved since repeat SRS and completion of the dexamethasone taper), chest pain, palpitations, nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, mucositis, rash (she had a history of grade 2 acneiform rash on osimertinib that largely resolved; she notes very mild facial dryness currently), visual changes, or lower extremity swelling. The peripheral neuropathy in her bilateral feet (grade 2 at its peak during carboplatin/pemetrexed) has been stable and perhaps slightly improved on duloxetine -- she rates it as mild tingling, no longer affecting her gait.

She denies any fever, chills, or infectious symptoms. No hematuria, epistaxis, or other hemorrhagic events (she was on bevacizumab until second progression and that was discontinued). She is eating adequately and her weight is stable (she reports a slight weight loss of approximately 2 lbs since her last visit, which she attributes to reduced appetite with the new regimen).

Her daughter notes that the patient seems more engaged and motivated since starting the trial, and reports the patient has been going for daily short walks.

**REVIEW OF SYSTEMS**
Positive: Grade 1 fatigue; mild facial skin dryness; bilateral foot tingling (stable, pre-existing, related to prior platinum); mild appetite reduction; right frontal headache (improved from baseline at progression, now mild).
Negative: No fever, chills, night sweats. No new headache or neurological symptoms. No visual disturbance. No cough (notably improved from initial presentation; patient notes her cough resolved on osimertinib and has not returned). No hemoptysis. No progressive dyspnea. No pleuritic chest pain. No nausea or vomiting. No diarrhea or constipation. No mucositis or oral sores. No new skin rash beyond mild dryness. No arthralgias or myalgias. No lower extremity edema. No dysuria or hematuria. No new musculoskeletal complaints.

**PHYSICAL EXAMINATION**
Vital Signs: Blood pressure 128/78 mmHg (well-controlled; patient is on lisinopril and amlodipine for hypertension, with amlodipine added during bevacizumab era), heart rate 74 beats per minute (regular), respiratory rate 16 breaths per minute, temperature 36.8 degrees Celsius, oxygen saturation 95% on room air (consistent with her known mild COPD baseline; SpO2 was 96% at prior visit), weight [recorded per protocol], BMI within normal range.

General: Well-appearing, well-nourished Chinese-American woman in no acute distress. Alert and oriented x3. Conversational, in good spirits. Accompanied by her daughter.

HEENT: Normocephalic, atraumatic. Pupils equal, round, and reactive to light bilaterally. Extraocular movements intact. No scleral icterus. Oropharynx clear, no mucositis, no oral candidiasis. Mild perioral skin dryness noted. No cervical or supraclavicular lymphadenopathy palpable.

Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. Normal S1 and S2. No murmurs, rubs, or gallops. No peripheral edema. Radial pulses 2+ bilaterally.

Pulmonary: Mild decreased breath sounds at the right lung base, unchanged from prior examination (consistent with known RUL mass and prior pleural effusion territory; pleural effusion resolved on prior imaging). No wheezing, crackles, or rhonchi. No signs of acute respiratory distress.

Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended. No hepatosplenomegaly. No palpable abdominal masses. Active bowel sounds.

Skin: Mild seborrheic-like facial dryness without active papules or pustules. No grade 2 or higher rash. Bilateral first toenails show residual changes from prior paronychia, healing. No new skin lesions.

Neurological: Alert and fully oriented. Cranial nerves II-XII grossly intact. No focal motor or sensory deficits in upper extremities. Mild symmetric decreased sensation to light touch in bilateral distal feet (toes and plantar surface), consistent with known grade 1-2 peripheral neuropathy. Gait steady. No ataxia. No cerebellar findings on examination today (previously, right cerebellar metastasis was small and now resolved/treated). Coordination intact (finger-nose testing normal).

Musculoskeletal: No joint swelling or tenderness. Full range of motion of major joints. No bone pain elicited on palpation of spine or long bones.

Lymphatics: No palpable peripheral lymphadenopathy in axillary, inguinal, or remaining cervical chains.

**LABORATORY RESULTS**
Complete Blood Count: WBC 5.8 x10^9/L (within normal limits); Hemoglobin 10.8 g/dL (grade 1 anemia, persistent from prior carboplatin-related anemia; stable compared to last check of 10.4 g/dL during platinum-based therapy, slight improvement); Platelets 198 x10^9/L (normal); Neutrophils 3.4 x10^9/L (ANC within normal limits, no neutropenia).

Comprehensive Metabolic Panel: Sodium 138 mEq/L, Potassium 4.0 mEq/L, Chloride 103 mEq/L, CO2 24 mEq/L, BUN 16 mg/dL, Creatinine 0.82 mg/dL (eGFR >60, renal function preserved), Glucose 108 mg/dL (fasting, consistent with known type 2 DM), Total protein 6.9 g/dL, Albumin 3.8 g/dL, Total bilirubin 0.6 mg/dL, AST 28 U/L (normal), ALT 32 U/L (normal), Alkaline phosphatase 74 U/L (normal).

LFTs and renal function reassuring -- no hepatotoxicity or nephrotoxicity signal from study drug at cycle 1. Electrolytes stable. Albumin adequate.

Additional per-protocol labs: LDH 198 U/L (borderline elevated, likely reflecting disease burden, not significantly changed from prior); HbA1c 7.1% (acceptable diabetes control); TSH 2.1 mIU/L (normal, obtained given anti-PD-L1 component of trial -- baseline thyroid function within normal limits, no immunotherapy-related thyroid dysfunction to date); Urinalysis: no proteinuria (dipstick negative; bevacizumab discontinued, but protocol requires continued monitoring given prior exposure and trial drug combination).

Coagulation studies: PT/INR and aPTT within normal limits.

Cardiac monitoring: 12-lead ECG obtained per protocol: normal sinus rhythm, rate 74 bpm, normal intervals (QTc 418 ms, within acceptable range per protocol threshold), no ST-T wave changes. Echocardiogram performed at screening was within normal limits (LVEF 60%); no repeat echo required at this visit per protocol.

**IMAGING RESULTS**
No new imaging obtained at this visit. Next protocol-mandated restaging CT chest/abdomen/pelvis and MRI brain are scheduled for after cycle 3 (approximately 6 weeks from the start of treatment, so roughly 2 more weeks from today). At the time of trial enrollment, the most recent CT chest showed a 3.8 cm right upper lobe mass, bilateral pulmonary nodules (RLL 0.8 cm, LLL 0.6 cm), and the MRI brain showed the recurrent right frontal lesion at 1.4 cm (now treated with repeat SRS). Baseline imaging is formally documented in the study chart.

The patient notes her headache is significantly better since repeat SRS and dexamethasone taper. She is neurologically intact on exam today, which is reassuring given the right frontal location of the treated lesion.

**ASSESSMENT**
Ms. Margaret Yuen is a 64-year-old woman with EGFR L858R/T790M co-mutant, MET-amplified advanced pulmonary adenocarcinoma, currently on cycle 1 of an investigational EGFR/MET bispecific antibody plus anti-PD-L1 clinical trial. She is at her C1W4 safety visit.

1. Disease status: Too early to assess tumor response at this visit. Restaging imaging is scheduled at the end of cycle 2 / beginning of cycle 3 per protocol. Subjectively, she reports slight improvement in her headaches and no worsening dyspnea, which may represent early favorable signals, but this cannot be attributed to treatment with certainty at this time. Her performance status is stable at ECOG 2 at the time of enrollment; today she appears clinically at ECOG 1-2 (she is more functional than she appeared at the urgent visit 4-5 weeks ago, and she is walking daily per her daughter). This is encouraging.

2. Tolerability: Cycle 1 was tolerated reasonably well. The C1D1 mild infusion-related reaction (grade 1) was managed appropriately per protocol with antihistamines and rate reduction, and she did not experience recurrence at subsequent infusions this cycle. Grade 1 fatigue is present and acceptable. No evidence of hepatotoxicity, nephrotoxicity, pneumonitis, or immune-mediated adverse events on cycle 1 labs.

3. Residual toxicities from prior therapy: Grade 1 peripheral neuropathy from prior platinum is stable and slightly improved on duloxetine. Mild residual anemia (grade 1, Hgb 10.8) from prior chemotherapy; no intervention required at this level, will continue to monitor. Hypertension well-controlled on current regimen.

4. Intracranial disease: Right frontal recurrence was treated with repeat SRS approximately 5-6 weeks ago. Clinically improved -- headache resolved, no neurologic deficits. Restaging MRI brain at cycle 3 will formally assess response at the SRS site. Right cerebellar lesion remained stable/resolved and was not re-treated.

5. Performance status: ECOG 1-2 (clinically trending toward ECOG 1 compared to ECOG 2 at enrollment 4 weeks ago). Patient remains fully engaged in her care, is eating and ambulating. No immediate concern for imminent clinical deterioration.

6. Diabetes management: Glucose and HbA1c stable. No changes to diabetes regimen needed at this time. Will continue to monitor.

7. Social/psychosocial: Patient and family remain highly engaged. Social work has been involved to provide support services given the patient's functional decline over the course of her disease. Patient's daughter is an important caregiver and advocate and was present today; family dynamics appear supportive. The patient's mood appeared notably improved today compared to the visit at second progression, which is a positive sign.

**PLAN**

1. Continue protocol treatment per study schedule. Cycle 2 day 1 dosing to proceed as planned. Pre-medicate per updated protocol (diphenhydramine 25 mg IV and dexamethasone 8 mg IV prior to each infusion) given prior grade 1 infusion-related reaction.

2. Restaging CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast and MRI brain with gadolinium to be obtained after cycle 2 completion, per protocol (approximately 6 weeks from treatment initiation). These images will constitute the first formal on-treatment tumor assessment.

3. Continue supportive care:
   - Duloxetine 60 mg daily for peripheral neuropathy (continue current dose, reassess at next visit)
   - Lisinopril and amlodipine for hypertension; blood pressure today well-controlled
   - Metformin for diabetes management; glycemic control acceptable
   - Iron supplementation for grade 1 anemia; recheck CBC at cycle 2 day 1
   - Lubricating eye drops as needed for residual dry eye (noted during osimertinib era, mild, persistent)

4. Protocol-required monitoring:
   - CBC, CMP, LFTs, thyroid function at each cycle day 1 visit
   - ECG for QTc monitoring per protocol
   - Urinalysis with each cycle
   - Toxicity assessment per NCI CTCAE v5.0 at each visit

5. No dose modifications required at this time. All toxicities are grade 1 and within acceptable protocol parameters.

6. If she develops any new neurological symptoms prior to the next visit (seizure, focal weakness, new headache, visual change), she is instructed to call the clinic immediately and present to the emergency department, given her history of brain metastases and recent SRS.

7. Social work to continue follow-up for supportive services. Daughter to remain involved as caregiver. Patient counseled on importance of adhering to the trial visit schedule and contacting the research team for any new symptoms.

8. Return to clinic in 3 weeks for cycle 2, day 1 dosing and safety assessment.

Case reviewed by the treating attending thoracic oncologist. Patient expressed understanding of the plan and verbalized agreement to proceed. All questions answered. The patient and her daughter were thanked for their continued partnership in care.

Total time spent on this encounter: 45 minutes, more than half of which was devoted to counseling, coordination of care, and medical decision-making.

Cancer status: Present, on active treatment. Response to current therapy: Not yet evaluable (too early; first restaging imaging pending).
